 The feasibility and acceptability of including specific variables (e.g. genetic data).
 How individuals with different risk profiles are categorised (e.g. number of groups/cutoff points).
 The tool’s user interface and output presentation, with reference to examples provided.
 Implications for clinical practice and future postdoctoral work.
To enhance end-user generalisability, I will conduct ≥3 focus groups for healthcare professionals recruited from
across the UK. The groups will be divided based on background and experience to minimise power imbalance. I
will also conduct ≥3 focus groups for people with psoriasis recruited from the Psoriasis Association and
dermatology clinics. I will leverage our skin research inclusion toolkit (manuscript in preparation) to ensure
representation from underserved groups, including lower socioeconomic classes, ethnic minorities and those with
disabilities.
All discussions will be audio-recorded and transcribed verbatim to enable inductive thematic analysis using NVivo
software. A systematic approach will be used to organise collected data into relevant codes, which will be grouped
into potential themes using an iterative pro

ul feedback for the proposal’s Plain English Summary.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
PPI is integral to the success of my research, particularly given its potential to provide more autonomy and
flexibility for individuals likely to sustain disease control long-term. To this end, I will establish a Patient and Lay
Advisory Group of 6 individuals, including people with psoriasis and their carers/family members. Individuals will
be recruited from my initial PPI session, the Psoriasis Association and the well-established inflammatory skin
disease PPI network led by my supervisors at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology. This will ensure a diversity of
views, backgrounds and experiences.
The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings around key decision points. Based on feedback, meetings
will be virtual to minimise disruption to daily schedules and maximise inclusivity. To reduce the potential for digital
exclusion, I will arrange practice online calls and provide phone-in options.
PPI collaborators will:
 Provide input on variabl

e selection for model development in Phase 2.
 Inform recruitment strategies for Phase 3, especially for underserved groups.
 Help design a topic guide and co-facilitate the focus groups in Phase 3.
 Assist with interpreting research findings and opine on their relevance to people with psoriasis and the
public.
Additionally, they will review communications on research findings intended for patients and the public. Guided by
the NIHR equality, diversity and inclusion toolkit, these will be widely disseminated via charities, established
European patient networks and partners, newsletters and social media. I will also leverage the annual national PPI
webinars co-organised by the Psoriasis Association and St John’s DermAcademy (>100 attendees per event) to
share my results. Where appropriate, I will invite PPI collaborators to co-author peer-reviewed publications and to
co-present at events.
PPI collaborators will be invited to attend PPI training sessions run by Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Trust and by the
NIHR Imperial College and Partners Research Support Service Hub. For those involved in Phase 3, I will provide
additional training on conducting focus groups and interpreting qualit

evidence.text:
Background
Psoriasis is a common skin condition that affects 1 in 50 people in the UK. It causes red scaly patches on the skin
and there is currently no known cure. For people with severe psoriasis, tablet or injection treatments are often
used to help them get it under control.
Newer injectable treatments called ‘biologics’ work better than older treatments and have helped many more
people get their psoriasis under control initially. Unfortunately, not everyone can keep it under control in the long-
term, even if they stay on treatment.
Currently, we cannot predict if a person will keep their psoriasis under control when they stay on a particular
treatment. For people with psoriasis, not knowing how long a particular treatment will work for can be extremely
worrying. It also means that everyone on treatment must attend regular hospital check-ups, even if they do not
actually need them. Limited NHS resources are therefore being used inefficiently, and people who need
appointments may struggle to get one.
Aim
To create a tool to identify people who are more or less likely to keep their psoriasis under control while receiving a
tablet or injection treatment. This could help to person
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
alise their long-term care and improve their treatment
experience.
Methods
First, I will review existing research to see how common it is for people to keep their psoriasis under control on
tablet or injection treatments. I will also look for individual factors, such as age and weight, which could make this
more or less likely.
Using two large datasets from the UK and Europe, I will then build computer models to predict how likely it is for
someone to keep their psoriasis under control. This prediction will be based on routinely collected clinical data,
such as age, ethnicity and smoking history. I will also look at whether including genetic data (i.e. information stored
in DNA) improves how well the models perform.
Finally, I will host separate group discussions with healthcare professionals (including doctors and nurses) and
people with psoriasis. They will provide input on which models would be best suited for use as a tool. They will
also help to make the tool more user-friendly. After creating the tool, I will invite healthcare professionals to test it,
and further improvements will be made based on their feedback.
Patient and Public Involvement
The problem this project aims t

criteria.name: Evidence Review Quality & Relevance
criteria.definition: How comprehensively and critically the application reviews existing literature and evidence.

signals.signal_text: Number and relevance of references cited
Presence of critical analysis vs. mere summary
Identification of knowledge gaps
Coherence and logical flow

criteria.pros: The application provides a critical and comprehensive review of existing literature, citing numerous specific studies (e.g., BADBIR, Cochrane reviews) and identifying clear limitations such as small sample sizes and lack of validation. It explicitly identifies the knowledge gap regarding the inability to predict long-term disease control, justifying the proposed systematic review and modeling.
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the evidence review is robust and directly informs the research questions.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the evidence review is robust and directly informs the research questions.

evidence.text:
ent. Despite psoriasis being one of the most common
IMIDs, no tools currently exist to predict the likelihood of sustaining disease control on systemic therapy. My
project will address this gap, with potential to:
 Boost patient/clinician satisfaction by reducing uncertainty.
 Empower individuals to address modifiable risk factors.
 Personalise long-term management strategies, aligning with the NHS Long Term Plan21.
 Inform participant selection for future trials, e.g. exploring dose minimisation strategies for cohorts likely to
sustain disease control.
3. Literature
3.1. How is disease control defined in psoriasis?
The NICE psoriasis guidelines define disease control as a Physician’s Global Assessment (PGA) of clear/almost
clear skin5. This correlates to a Psoriasis Area and Severity Index (PASI) ≤2 or a PASI 90 response (i.e. 90%
baseline)4, psoriasis5.
improvement from where PASI is the gold standard disease severity measure in
Achievement of disease control is typically evaluated 2-6 months post-initiation of systemic therapy22. Whether
this is sustained long-term (i.e. ≥6 months) is a separate, important issue, which my project will focus on. Loss of
disease control (i.e.

relapse) is defined as a ≥3 increase in PASI, with a minimum PASI of 523.
3.2. How feasible is sustained disease control on systemic therapy?
The Cochrane living systematic review comparing different systemic therapies only focused on randomised
controlled trial (RCT) data ≤6 months post-initiation22. However, open-label extensions (OLE) to RCTs have shed
light on the proportion sustaining longer-term disease control. After starting ixekizumab (IL-17 inhibitor), 75% and
73% of individuals exhibited disease control at 3 and 13 months24. In individuals who achieve disease control on
risankizumab (IL-23 inhibitor) at 7 months post-initiation, 87% and 81% maintained this at 12 and 24 months25.
Comparisons of longer-term RCT data are hindered by heterogeneity of reporting timepoints and trial design.
Additionally, the demographics and complexity of trial populations differ from those seen in clinical practice26. To
date, only one small-scale North American real-world registry study (n=687) has been published, indicating that a
lower proportion of individuals sustain disease control on biologics outside of trial settings (73%/60%/50% at
6/12/18 months respectively)27.
Although real-world

drug persistence studies do not directly address the proportion sustaining disease control,
they provide invaluable insight into longer-term effectiveness and safety. Data from the British Association of
Dermatologists Biologics and Immunomodulators Register (BADBIR) demonstrates that 46% of individuals remain
on methotrexate at 12 months28, considerably lower than for those treated with biologics (75%-88% depending on
the drug29). This highlights that drug choice may impact the likelihood of sustaining disease control.
Despite multiple studies looking at the proportion of individuals sustaining long-term psoriasis control, their clinical
utility remains limited due to there being no comprehensive summary synthesising the key findings. My project will
address this through a systematic review.
3.3. What clinical factors are associated with sustained disease control?
The rapidly growing cohort achieving initial psoriasis control is a recent phenomenon. As such, researching clinical
factors associated with sustained disease control has only become a priority of late, with few studies to date
addressing this directly. These include:
 A North American real-world registry study (n=687)

27, which identified skin itch (visual analogue scale,
VAS ≥30/100) or skin pain (VAS ≥45/100) to be associated with increased relapse risk on biologics
(HR=1.47; 95% CI 1.06-2.03). Conversely, being biologic-naïve at baseline was associated with reduced
risk (HR=0.78; 95% CI 0.62-0.98).
 An analysis of guselkumab (IL-23 inhibitor) trial data (n=494)30, which found diabetes (OR=0.23; 95% CI
0.05-0.98) and higher baseline disease impact (OR=0.94; 95% CI 0.89-0.99) to be associated with lower
odds of sustaining disease control. In contrast, higher week 4 guselkumab plasma concentrations were
associated with increased odds (OR=1.43, 95% CI 1.07-1.90).
However, both studies are limited by small sample sizes, wide confidence intervals and a lack of validation in
independent datasets.
Further insight can be gleaned from studies investigating different, but related, outcomes in psoriasis. A meta-
analysis of factors predicting biologic persistence31 identified female sex (HR=1.22; 95% CI 1.07-1.38) and obesity
(HR=1.21, 95% CI 1.10-1.32) to be associated with treatment discontinuation, while concomitant psoriatic arthritis
was associated with a decreased likelihood of this (HR=0.83, 95%

7. Further research is required
to establish whether these factors are also relevant in psoriasis.
Collectively, the literature suggests that risk stratification is possible for my target cohort. By incorporating both the
direct and indirect research above, and applying their findings to large real-world datasets, I aim to build a clinically
relevant risk stratification tool for routine care.
3.4. What are the genetic factors associated with sustained disease control?
There is a strong genetic component to psoriasis development, with previous research highlighting HLA-C*06:02
as a major risk factor for psoriasis susceptibility and severity38. HLA-C*06:02 also influences initial response to
biologics, with a BADBIR study demonstrating HLA-C*06:02-negative patients to be significantly more likely to
respond to adalimumab than ustekinumab (OR=2.95, 95% CI 1.93-4.51 at 6 months)38. Additionally, a recent
scoping review by our group highlighted HLA-C*06:02 as the most extensively studied genetic factor associated
with systemic treatment response in psoriasis39, although further validation is needed prior to its routine use in
isolation. Given that initial treatment response is a pre-req

criteria.name: Research Scope & Award Appropriateness
criteria.definition: Whether the project scope matches the scale and expectations of the proposed award (doctoral, postdoctoral, etc.).

signals.signal_text: Alignment of aims with award level
Feasibility indicators (time, complexity, tasks)
Proportion of work relative to award expectations

criteria.pros: The project scope is well-aligned with a 36-month doctoral fellowship, featuring three distinct phases (review, modeling, tool development). Feasibility is strongly supported by secured access to large datasets (BADBIR, HPOS), detailed sample size calculations, and a realistic timeline with contingency plans for recruitment.
criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the scope is ambitious yet feasible given the resources and prior data access.
criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the scope is ambitious yet feasible given the resources and prior data access.

evidence.text:
control. However, ongoing uncertainty over individual likelihoods of sustaining disease control
has prevented their full benefits from being realised in real-world settings. Anxiety over potential relapse remains
an issue, contributing to the persistence of legacy one-size-fits-all healthcare models designed for an era reliant on
less effective treatments requiring more intensive monitoring.
My proposed research could deliver a step change in longer-term psoriasis management, improving treatment
satisfaction and service efficiency. With the fast-growing adoption of targeted therapies for other IMIDs42, my
findings may also have broader impact beyond psoriasis.
4. Aims and objectives
My overarching aim is to develop a risk stratification tool to help tailor management for individuals with psoriasis
who have achieved disease control on systemic therapy, thereby improving long-term outcomes.
To do this, I will:
 Conduct a systematic review to evaluate published evidence on the proportion of individuals
sustaining disease control over time and the factors associated with this (Phase 1).
 Model the likelihood of sustaining disease control on systemic therapy using large-scale real-
wo

rld datasets (Phase 2).
 Optimise the predictive models for use as a risk stratification tool, with patient and clinician input
(Phase 3).
5. Project Plan
5.1. Phase 1: Systematic review (0-9 months)
Aim
Evaluate published evidence on the proportion of individuals who sustain psoriasis control on systemic therapy
over time, and the factors associated with this.
Research questions
 5.1a – What proportion of individuals sustain psoriasis control on systemic therapy over time?
 5.1b – What clinical/genetic factors are associated with sustained psoriasis control on systemic therapy?
Design
I will conduct a systematic review in accordance with PRISMA guidelines43. A comprehensive search for primary
studies will be performed in Medline, EMBASE, Web of Science and the Cochrane Skin Group Specialised
Register. The protocol will be registered on PROSPERO.
I will leverage our group’s collaboration with the Cochrane Skin Group44, through which I will access a wealth of
resources which could contribute to this review. These include:
 Completed data extraction for 167 RCTs with 12-month efficacy data, as part of a previous project
evaluating study design heterogeneity45.
 Ongoing data extr

sting) will improve model performance further. Methods including nested cross validation will be used for
internal validation and to prevent overfitting.
The HPOS dataset will be used for external validation. Model performance will be assessed using measures of
discrimination (C-statistic) and calibration (calibration slope, calibration-in-the-large).
Approach B
Using all clinical variables within the BADBIR dataset, I will fit, internally validate, and assess model performance
using the methods outlined above. This analysis will also be repeated, stratifying by drug class, to evaluate model
stability across different treatments.
Approach C
After filtering the BADBIR dataset to only include individuals with genetic data available, I will fit a penalised mixed-
effects multivariable logistic regression model as outlined in Approach B. I will then incorporate data on HLA-
C*06:02 and the polygenic risk score (see Section 3.4) individually to determine whether this improves model
performance. This analysis will be repeated, stratifying by drug class.
Sample size considerations
Using methods described by Riley et al. 201956, the expected sample of c. 9,500 individuals from BADBIR is
su

evidence.text:
action from RCTs and observational studies for a systematic review of serious adverse
events, which I will be supporting.
Eligible studies will meet the following criteria:
 Study design – RCTs (including OLE) and observational studies.
 Population – adults ≥18 years with psoriasis.
 Intervention – continued treatment with methotrexate or a biologic (TNF inhibitor, IL-12/IL-23 inhibitor, IL-
17 inhibitor, IL-23 inhibitor) at least until the primary outcome is measured.
 Comparator – any psoriasis treatment, placebo or no comparator.
 Primary outcome – proportion with sustained disease control (i.e. PGA clear/almost clear, PASI ≤2, or a
PASI 90 response) at 6 months after it is first achieved. In my PPI meeting, participants emphasised the
importance of longer-term control (i.e. ≥6 months).
 Secondary outcomes – proportion with sustained disease control at later timepoints, effect sizes for
clinical/genetic factors associated with sustained disease control, and proportion with serious adverse
events.
Quality assessment
tool46 tool47
Risk of bias will be assessed using the ROBINS-I for cohort studies/OLE and the ROB2 for RCTs.
Data analysis
Data from extracted studies will be d

udy53, with similar assessments
performed at baseline and follow-up. Access is available through the BIOMAP consortium54, a European
collaboration coordinated by Professor Smith.
Data analysis
RQ5.2a.
Baseline characteristics of included participants will be summarised descriptively. Kaplan-Meier survival curves will
show the proportion sustaining disease control over time. Observations will be censored at loss to follow-up or at 3
years post-registration, whichever is sooner. Using BADBIR data only, I will repeat this analysis stratifying by drug
class (methotrexate, TNF inhibitor, IL-12/IL-23 inhibitor, IL-17 inhibitor, IL-23 inhibitor).
Both unadjusted and adjusted estimates will be reported. Unadjusted estimates from the Kaplan-Meier analysis
will enable comparison with previous studies27,30, and adjusted estimates will evaluate the impact of key
sociodemographic variables (age/sex/ethnicity/location) on the unadjusted estimates. Adjusted estimates will be
based on discrete-time cloglog survival curves, to determine the proportion sustaining disease control at 6-monthly
intervals for up to 3 years. To account for repeated follow-ups within individuals, a random effect will be u

oach B will use clinical variables in the more comprehensive BADBIR dataset. This will result in
a more complex predictive model which will be internally validated. This has the advantage of evaluating a
broader range of variables compared to Approach A.
 Approach C will focus on the subset of individuals in BADBIR with clinical and genetic data, to
explore whether addition of genetic data improves clinical predictive model performance.
This study will be reported according to the TRIPOD guidelines55.
Approach A
Using clinical variables common to both BADBIR and HPOS, a penalised mixed-effects multivariable logistic
regression model will be fit to the BADBIR dataset to predict the likelihood of sustained disease control for the next
6 months, controlling for length of time on treatment. A random effect will be included as individuals will contribute
repeated measures of disease control at 6-monthly intervals. Missing data will be dealt with as in RQ5.2a.
Penalisation will use elastic net and lasso approaches to select the minimum subset of variables. I will also explore
whether additional machine learning approaches (e.g. support vector machine, random forest, extreme gradient
boo

sting) will improve model performance further. Methods including nested cross validation will be used for
internal validation and to prevent overfitting.
The HPOS dataset will be used for external validation. Model performance will be assessed using measures of
discrimination (C-statistic) and calibration (calibration slope, calibration-in-the-large).
Approach B
Using all clinical variables within the BADBIR dataset, I will fit, internally validate, and assess model performance
using the methods outlined above. This analysis will also be repeated, stratifying by drug class, to evaluate model
stability across different treatments.
Approach C
After filtering the BADBIR dataset to only include individuals with genetic data available, I will fit a penalised mixed-
effects multivariable logistic regression model as outlined in Approach B. I will then incorporate data on HLA-
C*06:02 and the polygenic risk score (see Section 3.4) individually to determine whether this improves model
performance. This analysis will be repeated, stratifying by drug class.
Sample size considerations
Using methods described by Riley et al. 201956, the expected sample of c. 9,500 individuals from BADBIR is

criteria.name: Potential Impact on Patients, Public, and Health Services
criteria.definition: Likely benefits or influence of the research on patients, carers, or health and care systems.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of direct or indirect patient/public benefits
Alignment with health priorities or societal needs
Clarity and specificity of expected outcomes

criteria.pros: The application clearly articulates direct patient benefits (reduced anxiety, increased autonomy) and health service impacts (cost savings, efficient resource allocation via PIFU pathways). The expected outcomes are specific, actionable, and aligned with NHS priorities for personalized care.
criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the impact pathway is clear and well-supported.
criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the impact pathway is clear and well-supported.

evidence.text:
Background
Psoriasis is a common skin condition that affects 1 in 50 people in the UK. It causes red scaly patches on the skin
and there is currently no known cure. For people with severe psoriasis, tablet or injection treatments are often
used to help them get it under control.
Newer injectable treatments called ‘biologics’ work better than older treatments and have helped many more
people get their psoriasis under control initially. Unfortunately, not everyone can keep it under control in the long-
term, even if they stay on treatment.
Currently, we cannot predict if a person will keep their psoriasis under control when they stay on a particular
treatment. For people with psoriasis, not knowing how long a particular treatment will work for can be extremely
worrying. It also means that everyone on treatment must attend regular hospital check-ups, even if they do not
actually need them. Limited NHS resources are therefore being used inefficiently, and people who need
appointments may struggle to get one.
Aim
To create a tool to identify people who are more or less likely to keep their psoriasis under control while receiving a
tablet or injection treatment. This could help to person
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
o address has been identified as a research priority by people with psoriasis, their
families and friends, and the healthcare professionals who treat them. The design of the project incorporates
further feedback from a meeting I had with people with psoriasis. For example, they emphasised that involving
patients and healthcare professionals from the start is essential for developing a useful tool. Patients and the
public will continue to be involved during this project through 6-monthly meetings. I will invite them to help plan the
group discussions and analyse the conversations after the sessions. They will also ensure the research is
inclusive and relevant to diverse communities.
Impact
This work will help people better understand how likely they are to keep their psoriasis under control while
receiving a tablet or injection treatment. It will also allow healthcare professionals to provide better care matched to
individual needs. This will improve the patient experience and allow healthcare resources to be used more
efficiently.
Dissemination
I will share my findings by presenting at conferences and by publishing in scientific journals. I will also work with
patients and the publ
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
uals with psoriasis are achieving disease control than ever before.
However, the extent to which disease control can be sustained long-term varies, with no reliable way of predicting
this currently. This uncertainty can cause significant anxiety9 and leads to a one-size-fits-all healthcare model of
regular follow-ups regardless of clinical need. This results in poorer treatment experiences, inefficient resource
allocation, and compounds the supply and demand mismatch in the NHS, making it harder for those in need to
access timely care6,10.
This project aims to develop a risk stratification tool to predict the likelihood of sustaining psoriasis control on
systemic therapy. Aligning with the NIHR strategic theme of Health Data Science, it will help personalise long-term
management strategies. For example:
 Individuals likely to sustain disease control will benefit from increased autonomy and flexibility, and may be
suitable for remote monitoring or patient-initiated follow-up (PIFU) pathways with less disruption to daily
life. This will also save costs and release system capacity10.
 Higher-risk individuals may be appropriate for adjunct therapies such as methotrexate, which may im

ructs of the theoretical framework of acceptability: affective attitude, burden, ethicality, intervention
coherence, opportunity cost, perceived effectiveness and self-efficacy60.
The tool will be further improved based on the beta testing outcomes, with repeat testing sessions organised as
required.
6. Dissemination/Outputs/Impact
6.1. Dissemination
Patients/Public
I will work with PPI collaborators to produce Plain English Summaries and infographics, which will be shared
through the BADBIR newsletter and international consortia with well-established patient networks
(HPOS/BIOMAP). Through our group’s collaboration with the Psoriasis Association, my findings will also be
disseminated via webinars and social media.
Clinicians/Researchers
My findings will be presented at national and international conferences, published in high-impact journals, and
shared via the St John’s DermAcademy platform. I will also use my supervisors’ networks to reach other
disciplines (e.g. rheumatology) where this work may be relevant.
Policymakers
My results will inform UK guideline updates via my publications and supervisors. Professor Smith chaired the NICE
psoriasis guidelines committee. She also lead

evidence.text:
alise their long-term care and improve their treatment
experience.
Methods
First, I will review existing research to see how common it is for people to keep their psoriasis under control on
tablet or injection treatments. I will also look for individual factors, such as age and weight, which could make this
more or less likely.
Using two large datasets from the UK and Europe, I will then build computer models to predict how likely it is for
someone to keep their psoriasis under control. This prediction will be based on routinely collected clinical data,
such as age, ethnicity and smoking history. I will also look at whether including genetic data (i.e. information stored
in DNA) improves how well the models perform.
Finally, I will host separate group discussions with healthcare professionals (including doctors and nurses) and
people with psoriasis. They will provide input on which models would be best suited for use as a tool. They will
also help to make the tool more user-friendly. After creating the tool, I will invite healthcare professionals to test it,
and further improvements will be made based on their feedback.
Patient and Public Involvement

Furthermore, this work may provide insights applicable to other IMIDs treated with systemic therapies (e.g.
eczema, arthritis).
2. Importance
2.1. Stakeholders have highlighted improved risk stratification as a key research priority
This project seeks to improve stratification of individuals who have already achieved disease control based on their
likelihood of sustaining it long-term. This aligns with 3 of the top 10 priorities in the 2018 James Lind Alliance
Priority Setting Partnership involving patients, carers and clinicians13:
 ‘What factors predict how well psoriasis responds to a treatment?’
 ‘Why do psoriasis treatments stop working well?’
 ‘What is the best way to treat sudden flare ups of psoriasis?’
People with active psoriasis often feel stigmatised and less confident due to its visual nature14. A global survey of
>8,000 reported that clear/almost clear skin empowers individuals to undertake activities including sw

iscrete risk groups?
 5.3c – What barriers and enablers affect the use of a risk stratification tool, and how acceptable is it to use
such a tool to guide psoriasis management?
Design
Phase 2 will produce a range of models designed to predict sustained disease control on systemic therapy,
differing in their complexity, accuracy, and included variables. In Phase 3, I will use focus groups to assess
stakeholder views on these models to inform subsequent tool development. The practicality of using the tool will
be evaluated in a beta testing workshop. All participants will be reimbursed57.
Focus groups
People with psoriasis and multidisciplinary healthcare professionals (dermatologists/pharmacists/nurses) will
attend separate online focus groups for open discussion on RQ5.3a-c. These will be 1.5 hours each, consist of 6-8
participants, and be co-facilitated by a trained member of my Patient and Lay Advisory Group. A topic guide
developed with PPI input will be used, with detailed probing followed by general questioning to achieve
convergence to group norms58.
Stakeholders will be shown the Phase 2 models, and their perspectives will inform:
 Acceptable levels of model performance fo

Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
I originally developed this proposal with the findings of the 2018 James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership13
involving patients, carers and clinicians in mind. This highlighted research into factors affecting sustained disease
control in psoriasis as a key unmet need, aligning with 3 of the top 10 priorities identified.
My proposal has since been refined further based on feedback from a PPI advisory group meeting I organised.
Participants were recruited from local networks and the Psoriasis Association, with diversity in terms of age,
ethnicity, geographical location and disease duration. They also had varied experiences of different topical and
systemic therapies for psoriasis.
All participants strongly supported my research question and felt that it was relevant. In particular, they
emphasised that:
 A better understanding of factors affecting sustained psoriasis control would reduce uncertainty for people
on systemic therapy.
 Identification of modifiable risk factors may empower individuals to make appropriate lifestyle changes.
 Personalised follow-up st

rategies co-designed by patients and clinicians are preferable to a blanket
approach.
Participants were enthusiastic about the idea of a risk stratification tool providing individualised estimates of the
likelihood of sustained disease control on systemic therapy. The group highlighted the following key points, which
have shaped my proposal:
 Disease control maintained over the longer term, i.e. ≥6 months, is especially important. Hence, Phases 1
and 2 focus on sustaining disease control for ≥6 months as a key outcome.
 Not everyone will be comfortable with genetic data being used. However, the group agreed that there is
value in exploring whether genetic data can improve model performance. Consequently, Phase 2 will
compare models including and excluding genetic data.
 To maximise the tool’s clinical utility, early involvement of patients and clinicians is essential. This
influenced Phase 3 of my proposal, in which stakeholder engagement is central to model refinement, user
interface design and beta testing.
Following this event, 4 individuals expressed an interest in continued involvement in the project as part of a Patient
and Lay Advisory Group. They have also provided helpf

e (AFP) and
an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship (ACF) in Dermatology. I have a proven track-record of successfully
balancing research with clinical training, having authored 14 peer-reviewed publications with 8 as first author (h-
index 9). I am well-equipped for this DRF due to my extensive research experience and strong organisational
skills.
Research Experience
During my AFP, I extracted data and assessed the risk of bias for two systematic reviews for the Cochrane
Tobacco Addiction Group, following a 4-day Training Programme organised by Cochrane UK. I also worked with
Professor Laura Coates, an academic rheumatologist, to design and run a multicentre study comparing different
patient-reported outcome measures in psoriatic arthritis. During this, I completed Good Clinical Practice training,
secured ethical approval, wrote the study documents, coordinated patient recruitment and analysed the data. I
presented my findings at the 2019 British Psoriatic Arthritis Consortium, winning the runner-up abstract award, with
two subsequent first author publications in The Journal of Rheumatology.
In parallel, I worked on a multicentre cohort study examining the association between immune-

related toxicity and
survival in metastatic melanoma patients treated with checkpoint immunotherapy. Supervised by Professor
Benjamin Fairfax, an academic oncologist, I led the project from conception to completion and became proficient in
using R for descriptive statistics and survival analysis. My work resulted in a joint first author publication in the
British Journal of Cancer. Separately, I characterised the cutaneous toxicities in this cohort, which led to an oral
presentation at the 2020 Annual Meeting of the British Association of Dermatologists (BAD) and a first author
publication in Clinical and Experimental Dermatology.
As part of my subsequent ACF in Dermatology, I completed a PGDip in Health Research with Distinction at the
University of Oxford. This provided in-depth training on study design, statistics, diagnostic and screening studies,
systematic reviews, and statistical computing using R and Stata. For my 9-month research rotation, I secured
£25,000 from Cancer Research UK. This funded single-cell RNA sequencing experiments to investigate gene
expression changes in melanoma patients who develop immune-related toxicities following checkpoint
immunotherapy. In additi

on to in-house training on peripheral mononuclear cell extraction, immune cell subset
isolation and single-cell sequencing, I attended courses on Linux for Genomics, RNA-seq data analysis and R to
complement my self-directed learning. My work contributed to a co-author publication in Nature Medicine and I
won two sought-after travel scholarships to present my research (European Congress of Rheumatology, World
Congress of Dermatology).
Since completing my ACF, I have immersed myself in full-time dermatology training while actively contributing to
research. I am a peer reviewer for the journals Skin Health and Disease and the British Journal of Dermatology
and have recently been awarded a prestigious part-time fellowship from the UK Dermatology Clinical Trials
Network (receiving the joint-highest score), where I will receive further training in patient-focused clinical research.
My diverse research experiences to date have helped me realise that my true passion lies in clinical research with
a direct impact on patient care. Being halfway through specialist training, I have experienced many different
subspecialties and identified inflammatory skin disease as an area combining my acade

criteria.name: Inclusive Research Design
criteria.definition: Consideration of inclusivity throughout the research lifecycle, including participant diversity and accessibility.

signals.signal_text: Explicit strategies for diverse recruitment
Adaptive methods or accessibility considerations
Equity and participation indicators

criteria.pros: The application explicitly plans for inclusivity using a 'skin research inclusion toolkit' to recruit underserved groups (lower socioeconomic classes, ethnic minorities, disabilities). PPI collaborators are tasked with ensuring the research is relevant to diverse communities, and accessibility is considered in tool design.
criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the inclusion strategy is specific and well-resourced.
criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the inclusion strategy is specific and well-resourced.

evidence.text:
o address has been identified as a research priority by people with psoriasis, their
families and friends, and the healthcare professionals who treat them. The design of the project incorporates
further feedback from a meeting I had with people with psoriasis. For example, they emphasised that involving
patients and healthcare professionals from the start is essential for developing a useful tool. Patients and the
public will continue to be involved during this project through 6-monthly meetings. I will invite them to help plan the
group discussions and analyse the conversations after the sessions. They will also ensure the research is
inclusive and relevant to diverse communities.
Impact
This work will help people better understand how likely they are to keep their psoriasis under control while
receiving a tablet or injection treatment. It will also allow healthcare professionals to provide better care matched to
individual needs. This will improve the patient experience and allow healthcare resources to be used more
efficiently.
Dissemination
I will share my findings by presenting at conferences and by publishing in scientific journals. I will also work with
patients and the publ
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
r clinical use.
 The feasibility and acceptability of including specific variables (e.g. genetic data).
 How individuals with different risk profiles are categorised (e.g. number of groups/cutoff points).
 The tool’s user interface and output presentation, with reference to examples provided.
 Implications for clinical practice and future postdoctoral work.
To enhance end-user generalisability, I will conduct ≥3 focus groups for healthcare professionals recruited from
across the UK. The groups will be divided based on background and experience to minimise power imbalance. I
will also conduct ≥3 focus groups for people with psoriasis recruited from the Psoriasis Association and
dermatology clinics. I will leverage our skin research inclusion toolkit (manuscript in preparation) to ensure
representation from underserved groups, including lower socioeconomic classes, ethnic minorities and those with
disabilities.
All discussions will be audio-recorded and transcribed verbatim to enable inductive thematic analysis using NVivo
software. A systematic approach will be used to organise collected data into relevant codes, which will be grouped
into potential themes using an iterative pro

ul feedback for the proposal’s Plain English Summary.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
PPI is integral to the success of my research, particularly given its potential to provide more autonomy and
flexibility for individuals likely to sustain disease control long-term. To this end, I will establish a Patient and Lay
Advisory Group of 6 individuals, including people with psoriasis and their carers/family members. Individuals will
be recruited from my initial PPI session, the Psoriasis Association and the well-established inflammatory skin
disease PPI network led by my supervisors at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology. This will ensure a diversity of
views, backgrounds and experiences.
The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings around key decision points. Based on feedback, meetings
will be virtual to minimise disruption to daily schedules and maximise inclusivity. To reduce the potential for digital
exclusion, I will arrange practice online calls and provide phone-in options.
PPI collaborators will:
 Provide input on variabl

e selection for model development in Phase 2.
 Inform recruitment strategies for Phase 3, especially for underserved groups.
 Help design a topic guide and co-facilitate the focus groups in Phase 3.
 Assist with interpreting research findings and opine on their relevance to people with psoriasis and the
public.
Additionally, they will review communications on research findings intended for patients and the public. Guided by
the NIHR equality, diversity and inclusion toolkit, these will be widely disseminated via charities, established
European patient networks and partners, newsletters and social media. I will also leverage the annual national PPI
webinars co-organised by the Psoriasis Association and St John’s DermAcademy (>100 attendees per event) to
share my results. Where appropriate, I will invite PPI collaborators to co-author peer-reviewed publications and to
co-present at events.
PPI collaborators will be invited to attend PPI training sessions run by Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Trust and by the
NIHR Imperial College and Partners Research Support Service Hub. For those involved in Phase 3, I will provide
additional training on conducting focus groups and interpreting qualit

sub_criteria.name: Quality and Relevance of Proposed Training
sub_criteria.definition: How well-structured, clear, and relevant the training plan is in relation to the project’s goals and the applicant’s research needs.

signals.signal_text: Completeness and clarity of training description
Alignment of training topics with project methods or techniques
Structured timeline or sequencing of training activities
Explicit links between training content and skill acquisition

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides a highly detailed, structured training plan covering all methodological areas required (systematic reviews, statistics, genetic epidemiology, qualitative methods, dashboard development, PPI, leadership). It explicitly names specific courses (e.g., LSHTM, UCL, Oxford), their duration, and the year they will be undertaken. The plan clearly links each training activity to specific skill acquisition needed for the project phases.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details found; the training plan is comprehensive and directly evidenced.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details found; the training plan is comprehensive and directly evidenced.

evidence.text:
Proposed training and development programme
I have designed the following bespoke training and development plan with the aid of my supervisors. This will
ensure I acquire the skills necessary to successfully deliver my proposal, and support my development as an
academic dermatologist.
Systematic Reviews
I have experience with data extraction and risk of bias assessments through co-authoring two Cochrane
systematic reviews. Additionally, I completed a 4-day Cochrane Review Author Training Course in 2018 and a
5-day Systematic Reviews course (Oxford University) in 2020.
I will build upon these foundations by attending a 4-day Systematic Reviews and Meta-analysis of Health
Research course (London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine) in my first year, which will provide a refresher
of systematic review methodology. This will be followed by a 3-day Introduction to Meta-analysis course (UCL),
which will provide a practical toolkit for meta-analysis and heterogeneity assessment using R.
In addition to formal courses, I will also be supported via our group’s collaboration with the Cochrane Skin Group.
Statistics and Computer Programming
I have developed a strong practical grounding in st
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
atistics over the past 6 years, with experience in summarising
data using descriptive statistics and conducting survival analyses (see research background). I am also familiar
with the RStudio environment and have significant prior experience in using R for data wrangling, visualisation and
statistical tests. This has prepared me well for this fellowship, as R will be the main programming language used
for data analysis.
I completed a 5-day Statistical Computing with R and Stata course in 2023 (Oxford University), which provided
an introduction to statistical principles relevant to this project, including dealing with missing data, power
calculations, regression modelling, and cross validation. While this gave me a basic understanding of different
statistical techniques, I would benefit from further formal training during this fellowship.
I will attend a 5-day course on Practical Statistics for Epidemiology using R (Oxford University) in my first year
to gain experience in interpreting and presenting statistical results, and learn techniques to address bias and
confounding. I will also complete a 2-day Regressions with R course (UCL) to learn how to fit different regression
models,
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
including logistic regression which is central to my analyses. To prepare for the predictive modelling
component of my proposal, I will attend a 3-day Statistical Methods for Risk Prediction and Prognostic
Models course (Birmingham University). This will improve my knowledge of statistical methods for developing and
validating predictive models. Additionally, I will receive dedicated hands-on training from Dr Niamh Dooley, a
statistician at KCL, and Dr Sam Norton, who will supervise the statistical components of my project.
Genetic Epidemiology
In Phase 2, I will explore whether the predictive performance of clinical models can be meaningfully improved by
incorporating genetic data. To better understand different genetic data types and the construction and use of
polygenic risk scores, I will attend a 5-day Genetic Epidemiology course (Bristol University).
I will also benefit from a collaboration with Professor Michael Simpson at the Department of Genetics in KCL, who
has significant expertise in this area.
Qualitative Research Methods
During my third year, I will conduct focus groups to collate stakeholder perspectives on the models developed. In
preparation, I will complete two
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
courses in my second year: a 1-day Introduction to doing Qualitative Interviews
course followed by a 5-day Introduction to Qualitative Research Methods course (both Oxford University).
These will yield vital knowledge on how to successfully conduct focus groups, and give me practice with coding
and thematic analysis. I will build on this by completing a part-time NVivo Core Skills Online Course (NVivo
Academy).
Dashboard Development
I plan to present the risk stratification tool as an interactive R Shiny dashboard. In my third year, I will complete the
Publishing Visualisations in R with Shiny and flexdashboard course (Johns Hopkins University via Coursera).
I will build on this by attending a 2-day Interactive Dashboards & Web Apps using R & Shiny course (Royal
Statistical Society). I will also leverage expertise available within our group, where a project is currently underway
to build an R Shiny dashboard to present pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic models of biologics.
PPI/E
Prior to starting the fellowship, I will have completed Public Involvement Training organised by the Patient
Experience Research Centre at Imperial College London. During the first year of my fellowship, I wi
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ll attend the
Online PPI training for researchers run by the NIHR Cambridge Biomedical Research Centre PPI team. I am
particularly interested in the sessions on building and maintaining a PPI group, using PPI to help communicate
research findings, evaluating PPI and planning inclusive PPI. In addition, I will build on my skills in public
communication by attending a 1-day Public Engagement course (Science Museum).
Our group has well-established links with the Psoriasis Association and a local inflammatory skin disease PPI
network. I will benefit from this extensive expertise in involving patient partners in research.
Leadership
In my first year, I will complete a 6-day Emerging Clinical Leaders course (The King’s Fund). This will provide
valuable leadership training which will maximise the success of this project. In my third year, I will attend a 3-day
Future Leaders Academy (European Society for Dermatological Research), focusing on delegation, conflict
resolution, and influencing strategic change. This presents a unique opportunity to connect with future leaders in
dermatology and receive guidance from experienced mentors, greatly benefitting my postdoctoral career.
Research Env

sub_criteria.name: Support for Career Aspirations
sub_criteria.definition: The extent to which the training plan, and any professional practice components, contribute to the applicant’s long-term career goals.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of career goals
Connections between training activities and future career trajectory
Indications of skill transferability beyond the current project
Reference to professional practice or experiential learning where applicable

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly states the goal to develop as an 'academic dermatologist' and links specific leadership courses (Emerging Clinical Leaders, Future Leaders Academy) to long-term career trajectory. The plan includes transferable skills (communication, teaching, career management) via the KCL Doctoral Development Programme and institutional support, ensuring skills extend beyond the immediate project.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the connection between training and career aspirations is explicit and robust.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the connection between training and career aspirations is explicit and robust.

evidence.text:
ironment
My supervisors have considerable experience supporting PhD students to completion and will provide expertise on
key methodological elements of this proposal. I will be hosted by the St John’s Institute of Dermatology, a world-
renowned centre of excellence in skin research with an outstanding track record of supporting clinical academic
PhD fellows. The KCL Doctoral Development Programme offers a comprehensive suite of training opportunities,
including courses on effective communication, teaching methods, and career management. Furthermore, the
frequent seminars and institute-wide events foster a vibrant environment conducive to collaboration and creativity.
Collectively, these factors make KCL an ideal home for this project and a nurturing space for my growth as an
academic dermatologist.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 1

Online PPI training for researchers run by the NIHR Cambridge Biomedical Research Centre PPI team. I am
particularly interested in the sessions on building and maintaining a PPI group, using PPI to help communicate
research findings, evaluating PPI and planning inclusive PPI. In addition, I will build on my skills in public
communication by attending a 1-day Public Engagement course (Science Museum).
Our group has well-established links with the Psoriasis Association and a local inflammatory skin disease PPI
network. I will benefit from this extensive expertise in involving patient partners in research.
Leadership
In my first year, I will complete a 6-day Emerging Clinical Leaders course (The King’s Fund). This will provide
valuable leadership training which will maximise the success of this project. In my third year, I will attend a 3-day
Future Leaders Academy (European Society for Dermatological Research), focusing on delegation, conflict
resolution, and influencing strategic change. This presents a unique opportunity to connect with future leaders in
dermatology and receive guidance from experienced mentors, greatly benefitting my postdoctoral career.

evidence.text:
Proposed training and development programme
I have designed the following bespoke training and development plan with the aid of my supervisors. This will
ensure I acquire the skills necessary to successfully deliver my proposal, and support my development as an
academic dermatologist.
Systematic Reviews
I have experience with data extraction and risk of bias assessments through co-authoring two Cochrane
systematic reviews. Additionally, I completed a 4-day Cochrane Review Author Training Course in 2018 and a
5-day Systematic Reviews course (Oxford University) in 2020.
I will build upon these foundations by attending a 4-day Systematic Reviews and Meta-analysis of Health
Research course (London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine) in my first year, which will provide a refresher
of systematic review methodology. This will be followed by a 3-day Introduction to Meta-analysis course (UCL),
which will provide a practical toolkit for meta-analysis and heterogeneity assessment using R.
In addition to formal courses, I will also be supported via our group’s collaboration with the Cochrane Skin Group.
Statistics and Computer Programming

ironment

toral Fellowship Application.
I confirm that St John’s Institute of Dermatology, part of King’s College London (KCL), supports this application
and that, if funded, the research and training will be supported and administered by KCL.
I confirm that Weiyu Ye will undertake this work and is eligible, for the duration of the award, to live and work in the
UK.
Dr Ye wishes to develop a clinical academic career and I confirm that I am aware of the principles and obligations
published by NIHR to support her clinical academic training and I confirm that KCL is committed to these principles
and obligations.
The St John’s Institute of Dermatology (School of Basic and Medical Biosciences, KCL) is an internationally
renowned centre for the delivery of innovative clinical care for patients with skin diseases, cutting-edge education,
training, and research. A recent independent RAND assessment ranked St John’s as the leading clinical and
research institution for dermatology in the UK. St John’s occupies 1,200 square metres of state-of-the-art
laboratory space (Guy’s Hospital campus) and is fully integrated into the Faculty of Life Sciences & Medicine's
School of Basic & Medical Biosciences. An
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
active programme of graduate education is pursued including research
training (PhD and MD) and a world-renowned taught course (MSc) in Clinical Dermatology. The Institute excels in
research training, with a >95% success record of its Integrated Academic Training Pathway Fellows obtaining
external funding from MRC/NIHR. In addition, St John's Institute of Dermatology plays a leading role in the training
of dermatologists, both in the UK and around the world. Research at St John’s targets 4 key areas: inflammatory
and autoimmune skin disorders (the key area of this research proposal), cutaneous oncology, genetic skin
disorders, and skin-environment interactions. Our research is supported by grants from a range of sources
including NIHR, MRC, The Wellcome Trust, EU, US Foundations, British Skin Foundation, national charities, and
commercial sources. Our department research income averages >£3m per year with a School average research
grant income of >£25m per year. The latest submission to the REF confirmed Clinical Medicine was independently
rated to have 90% of its research as world leading or internationally excellent (3 or 4*) and 84 % of impact as
world leading (4*). King’s Colleg

e London is part of King’s Health Partners, one of the UK’s five Academic Health
Sciences Centres, where world-class research, teaching and clinical practice are brought together for the benefit

Maudsley NHS Foundation Trusts, are among the highest rated Trusts in the country and compete on the world

sub_criteria.pros: The application details a highly structured supervisory team with four supervisors, each with specific roles and expertise aligned to the project (ss.2.a, ss.2.c). Dr. Mahil (primary) provides clinical and methodological oversight; Dr. Norton covers statistics; Prof. Smith and Prof. Coates provide domain expertise and dataset access. Explicit meeting frequencies are defined (weekly, fortnightly, monthly) (ss.2.d). The text confirms mentorship plans including training support, progress monitoring via an independent Progression Committee, and specific guidance on PPI and statistical methods (ss.2.b).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the supervisory framework is exceptionally detailed and well-supported.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the supervisory framework is exceptionally detailed and well-supported.

Name Dr Satveer Mahil
Institution St John’s Institute of Dermatology, King’s College London (KCL)
Position Consultant Dermatologist and Reader
Description of support provided
Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at KCL. Her career to date has followed the NIHR
Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical F
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ellowship which led to an MRC-
funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical
Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship.
Dr Mahil’s translational research focuses on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to
personalise management strategies for inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. She has a strong interest
in optimising the management of individuals with psoriasis who have sustained disease control. She is currently
leading an NIHR-funded multicentre feasibility randomised controlled trial comparing patient-led ‘as-needed’
biologic treatment, therapeutic drug monitoring-guided biologic treatment, and biologic continuation (standard
care) in this cohort. She has extensive experience across multiple methods which will be used in this fellowship,
including systematic reviews, observational dataset analysis and qualitative research. She is currently supervising
3 PhD students (all ongoing), including 1 NIHR DRF.
She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member
of British Associati
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
on of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is
director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform.
I have established an excellent working relationship with Dr Mahil throughout the development of this fellowship
proposal and she will be my primary supervisor. We will meet weekly to discuss progress, address unforeseen
challenges/barriers, and ensure my training needs are met.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 2
Name Dr Sam Norton
Institution King’s College London (KCL)
Position Reader in Research Methods and Statistics
Description of support provided
Dr Sam Norton is a Reader in Research Methods and Statistics at KCL. He has significant expertise in meta-
analysis, longitudinal data analysis, and working with large-scale observational cohort studies of chronic disease.
He is a KCL Clinical Trials Unit affiliated senior statistician and the capacity development lead for the NIHR
Research Support Service delivered by KCL and its partners. He was previously the statistical lead for the NIHR
Research Design Service London.
Dr Norton has considerable experience in the development of risk prediction tools and is currently
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
supervising an
NIHR DRF developing an infection prediction tool for people with immune mediated inflammatory diseases treated
with targeted therapies. He has close collaborations with Dr Mahil, Professor Smith and Professor Coates, with a
strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs.
I have worked closely with Dr Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research
proposal. Throughout the fellowship, he will directly supervise my statistical training to ensure I gain the necessary
skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal.
Dr Norton has supervised 21 doctoral students, of which 12 have completed and 9 are ongoing. He has
considerable experience supporting NIHR fellowships and is a supervisor or collaborator for 2 current NIHR DRFs,
1 NIHR ACF, and 1 completed NIHR DRF. We will aim to meet fortnightly during the fellowship to discuss progress
and ensure my training needs are met.

evidence.text:
tology and Therapeutics
Description of support provided
Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Investigator and Professor of Dermatology
and Therapeutics at KCL. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert,
with experience ranging from biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She sits on the Steering Committees
of the key datasets for this fellowship: the British Association of Dermatologists Biologics and Immunomodulators
Register (BADBIR), the Biomarkers and Stratification To Optimise outcomes in Psoriasis study (BSTOP), and the
HIPPOCRATES Prospective Observational Study (HPOS). Additionally, she is involved in major national and
international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, the EU BIOMAP consortium,
and is a board member of the International Psoriasis Council. She also has strong links to the Psoriasis
Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists
biologics guideline committee. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of
Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor.

5. Project Plan
5.1. Phase 1: Systematic review (0-9 months)
Aim
Evaluate published evidence on the proportion of individuals who sustain psoriasis control on systemic therapy
over time, and the factors associated with this.
Research questions
 5.1a – What proportion of individuals sustain psoriasis control on systemic therapy over time?
 5.1b – What clinical/genetic factors are associated with sustained psoriasis control on systemic therapy?
Design
I will conduct a systematic review in accordance with PRISMA guidelines43. A comprehensive search for primary
studies will be performed in Medline, EMBASE, Web of Science and the Cochrane Skin Group Specialised
Register. The protocol will be registered on PROSPERO.
I will leverage our group’s collaboration with the Cochrane Skin Group44, through which I will access a wealth of
resources which could contribute to this review. These include:
 Completed data extraction for 167 RCTs with 12-month efficacy data, as part of a previous project
evaluating study design heterogeneity45.
 Ongoing data extr

evidence.text:
e London is part of King’s Health Partners, one of the UK’s five Academic Health
Sciences Centres, where world-class research, teaching and clinical practice are brought together for the benefit
of patients. The three NHS partners; Guy’s and St Thomas’, King’s College Hospital (KCH) and South London and
Maudsley NHS Foundation Trusts, are among the highest rated Trusts in the country and compete on the world
stage and attract the best clinical academics to pioneer world-leading advances in health and well-being. Dr Weiyu
Ye will have full support from the extensive infrastructure within the Department, School, KCL, and King’s Health
Partners. We will ensure the equipment and facilities successfully allow Weiyu to pursue her research. We will also
provide extensive personal support and mentorship to allow Weiyu to fully develop her research ideas, and also to
embed her career as a future leading clinical academic in dermatology. As a host organization, KCL is committed
to creating and maintaining an inclusive and supportive research culture for all, championing equality, diversity and
inclusion, and research integrity. KCL has been a member of the Athena SWAN Charter since 2007 and
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
in 2021 it
received a Silver Award. In 2018, the Faculty of Life Sciences & Medicine was recognised with a Faculty Athena
SWAN Silver award as recognition of our ongoing commitment to achieving gender equality and an inclusive
environment.
Dr Ye’s project aims to develop a risk stratification tool to predict the likelihood of sustaining psoriasis control on
systemic therapy. This has the potential to facilitate more personalised long-term management for individuals who
have achieved psoriasis control, thereby improving patient outcomes. The project has 3 main workstreams:
1. Systematic review to evaluate published evidence on the proportion of individuals sustaining psoriasis
control over time on systemic therapy, and the factors associated with this.
2. Model the likelihood of sustaining disease control on systemic therapy using large-scale real-world
datasets.
3. Optimise the predictive models for use as a risk stratification tool, with patient and clinician input.
This work is integrated with the clinical academic activities of two Faculty members within the department,
Professor Catherine Smith and Dr Satveer Mahil, who will provide direct supervision, training and mentoring.

ellowship which led to an MRC-
funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical
Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship.
Dr Mahil’s translational research focuses on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to
personalise management strategies for inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. She has a strong interest
in optimising the management of individuals with psoriasis who have sustained disease control. She is currently
leading an NIHR-funded multicentre feasibility randomised controlled trial comparing patient-led ‘as-needed’
biologic treatment, therapeutic drug monitoring-guided biologic treatment, and biologic continuation (standard
care) in this cohort. She has extensive experience across multiple methods which will be used in this fellowship,
including systematic reviews, observational dataset analysis and qualitative research. She is currently supervising
3 PhD students (all ongoing), including 1 NIHR DRF.
She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member
of British Associati

on of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is

proposal and she will be my primary supervisor. We will meet weekly to discuss progress, address unforeseen
challenges/barriers, and ensure my training needs are met.

5. Project Plan
5.1. Phase 1: Systematic review (0-9 months)
Aim
Evaluate published evidence on the proportion of individuals who sustain psoriasis control on systemic therapy
over time, and the factors associated with this.
Research questions
 5.1a – What proportion of individuals sustain psoriasis control on systemic therapy over time?
 5.1b – What clinical/genetic factors are associated with sustained psoriasis control on systemic therapy?
Design
I will conduct a systematic review in accordance with PRISMA guidelines43. A comprehensive search for primary
studies will be performed in Medline, EMBASE, Web of Science and the Cochrane Skin Group Specialised
Register. The protocol will be registered on PROSPERO.
I will leverage our group’s collaboration with the Cochrane Skin Group44, through which I will access a wealth of
resources which could contribute to this review. These include:
 Completed data extraction for 167 RCTs with 12-month efficacy data, as part of a previous project
evaluating study design heterogeneity45.
 Ongoing data extr

UK.
Dr Ye wishes to develop a clinical academic career and I confirm that I am aware of the principles and obligations

The St John’s Institute of Dermatology (School of Basic and Medical Biosciences, KCL) is an internationally
renowned centre for the delivery of innovative clinical care for patients with skin diseases, cutting-edge education,
training, and research. A recent independent RAND assessment ranked St John’s as the leading clinical and
research institution for dermatology in the UK. St John’s occupies 1,200 square metres of state-of-the-art
laboratory space (Guy’s Hospital campus) and is fully integrated into the Faculty of Life Sciences & Medicine's

evidence.text:
in 2021 it
received a Silver Award. In 2018, the Faculty of Life Sciences & Medicine was recognised with a Faculty Athena
SWAN Silver award as recognition of our ongoing commitment to achieving gender equality and an inclusive
environment.
Dr Ye’s project aims to develop a risk stratification tool to predict the likelihood of sustaining psoriasis control on
systemic therapy. This has the potential to facilitate more personalised long-term management for individuals who
have achieved psoriasis control, thereby improving patient outcomes. The project has 3 main workstreams:
1. Systematic review to evaluate published evidence on the proportion of individuals sustaining psoriasis
control over time on systemic therapy, and the factors associated with this.
2. Model the likelihood of sustaining disease control on systemic therapy using large-scale real-world
datasets.
3. Optimise the predictive models for use as a risk stratification tool, with patient and clinician input.
This work is integrated with the clinical academic activities of two Faculty members within the department,
Professor Catherine Smith and Dr Satveer Mahil, who will provide direct supervision, training and mentoring.

evidence.text:
d evidence on the proportion of individuals sustaining disease control over time and the
factors associated with this.
 Model the likelihood of sustaining disease control on systemic therapy using real-world datasets.
 Optimise the predictive models for use as a risk stratification tool, with patient and clinician input.
Methods and Timelines
Phase 1 (Months 0-9): Conduct a systematic review to determine the proportion of individuals sustaining psoriasis
control on systemic therapy over time, and the clinical and genetic factors associated with this.
Phase 2 (Months 7-24): Develop and validate predictive models of sustained disease control on systemic therapy
using two large-scale real-world datasets from the UK and Europe to ensure generalisability. I will also explore
whether including genetic data improves model performance.
Phase 3 (Months 19-36): Refine the predictive models into a risk stratification tool. Patient and clinician focus
groups will determine clinically acceptable levels of model performance, influence the definition of risk categories,
and guide user interface design. The tool will be beta tested via a clinician workshop.
PPI feedback has been integral to deve

loping this proposal. PPI collaborators will be closely involved throughout,
including assisting with recruitment strategies, co-facilitating focus groups, and helping with qualitative analysis.
They will also ensure the research is inclusive and relevant to diverse communities.
Impact
Successful delivery of the risk stratification tool could reduce patient anxiety, empower individuals to address
modifiable risk factors, and personalise long-term management strategies. For example, individuals likely to
sustain disease control will benefit from increased autonomy and flexibility and may be suited to remote monitoring
or patient-initiated follow-up pathways. Higher-risk individuals may be appropriate for adjunct therapies with closer
monitoring, facilitating earlier intervention for flares to reduce disease impact.
Dissemination
My findings will be disseminated widely through high-impact peer-reviewed publications and presentations at
national and international conferences. I will co-develop Plain English Summaries and infographics with my PPI
collaborators, which will be shared via UK and European psoriasis patient associations, newsletters, and social
media platforms to maximise p

evidence.text:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
I originally developed this proposal with the findings of the 2018 James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership13
involving patients, carers and clinicians in mind. This highlighted research into factors affecting sustained disease
control in psoriasis as a key unmet need, aligning with 3 of the top 10 priorities identified.
My proposal has since been refined further based on feedback from a PPI advisory group meeting I organised.
Participants were recruited from local networks and the Psoriasis Association, with diversity in terms of age,
ethnicity, geographical location and disease duration. They also had varied experiences of different topical and
systemic therapies for psoriasis.
All participants strongly supported my research question and felt that it was relevant. In particular, they
emphasised that:
 A better understanding of factors affecting sustained psoriasis control would reduce uncertainty for people
on systemic therapy.
 Identification of modifiable risk factors may empower individuals to make appropriate lifestyle changes.
 Personalised follow-up st

ul feedback for the proposal’s Plain English Summary.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
PPI is integral to the success of my research, particularly given its potential to provide more autonomy and
flexibility for individuals likely to sustain disease control long-term. To this end, I will establish a Patient and Lay
Advisory Group of 6 individuals, including people with psoriasis and their carers/family members. Individuals will
be recruited from my initial PPI session, the Psoriasis Association and the well-established inflammatory skin
disease PPI network led by my supervisors at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology. This will ensure a diversity of
views, backgrounds and experiences.
The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings around key decision points. Based on feedback, meetings
will be virtual to minimise disruption to daily schedules and maximise inclusivity. To reduce the potential for digital
exclusion, I will arrange practice online calls and provide phone-in options.
PPI collaborators will:
 Provide input on variabl
