To enhance end-user generalisability, I will conduct ≥3 focus groups for healthcare professionals recruited from
across the UK. The groups will be divided based on background and experience to minimise power imbalance. I
will also conduct ≥3 focus groups for people with psoriasis recruited from the Psoriasis Association and
dermatology clinics. I will leverage our skin research inclusion toolkit (manuscript in preparation) to ensure
representation from underserved groups, including lower socioeconomic classes, ethnic minorities and those with
disabilities.
All discussions will be audio-recorded and transcribed verbatim to enable inductive thematic analysis using NVivo
software. A systematic approach will be used to organise collected data into relevant codes, which will be grouped
into potential themes using an iterative pro

ul feedback for the proposal’s Plain English Summary.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
PPI is integral to the success of my research, particularly given its potential to provide more autonomy and
flexibility for individuals likely to sustain disease control long-term. To this end, I will establish a Patient and Lay
Advisory Group of 6 individuals, including people with psoriasis and their carers/family members. Individuals will
be recruited from my initial PPI session, the Psoriasis Association and the well-established inflammatory skin
disease PPI network led by my supervisors at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology. This will ensure a diversity of
views, backgrounds and experiences.
The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings around key decision points. Based on feedback, meetings
will be virtual to minimise disruption to daily schedules and maximise inclusivity. To reduce the potential for digital
exclusion, I will arrange practice online calls and provide phone-in options.
PPI collaborators will:
 Provide input on variabl

e selection for model development in Phase 2.
 Inform recruitment strategies for Phase 3, especially for underserved groups.
 Help design a topic guide and co-facilitate the focus groups in Phase 3.
 Assist with interpreting research findings and opine on their relevance to people with psoriasis and the
public.
Additionally, they will review communications on research findings intended for patients and the public. Guided by
the NIHR equality, diversity and inclusion toolkit, these will be widely disseminated via charities, established
European patient networks and partners, newsletters and social media. I will also leverage the annual national PPI
webinars co-organised by the Psoriasis Association and St John’s DermAcademy (>100 attendees per event) to
share my results. Where appropriate, I will invite PPI collaborators to co-author peer-reviewed publications and to
co-present at events.
PPI collaborators will be invited to attend PPI training sessions run by Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Trust and by the
NIHR Imperial College and Partners Research Support Service Hub. For those involved in Phase 3, I will provide
additional training on conducting focus groups and interpreting qualit

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

sub_criteria.pros: Inclusivity is demonstrated across the full research lifecycle: PPI involvement spans proposal refinement, variable selection, recruitment strategies, focus group facilitation, and interpretation of results (secppi__001_b, secppi__001_c). The plan ensures relevance to diverse communities at every stage, from design to dissemination (secpesor__001_c, secsa__001_c).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The plan is very strong; the only minor limitation is that the specific mechanisms for ongoing evaluation and adaptation of inclusion strategies during the project execution are implied rather than explicitly detailed as a separate monitoring process.
sub_criteria.rationale: The plan is very strong; the only minor limitation is that the specific mechanisms for ongoing evaluation and adaptation of inclusion strategies during the project execution are implied rather than explicitly detailed as a separate monitoring process.

Impact
This work will help people better understand how likely they are to keep their psoriasis under control while
receiving a tablet or injection treatment. It will also allow healthcare professionals to provide better care matched to
individual needs. This will improve the patient experience and allow healthcare resources to be used more
efficiently.

loping this proposal. PPI collaborators will be closely involved throughout,
including assisting with recruitment strategies, co-facilitating focus groups, and helping with qualitative analysis.
They will also ensure the research is inclusive and relevant to diverse communities.
Impact
Successful delivery of the risk stratification tool could reduce patient anxiety, empower individuals to address
modifiable risk factors, and personalise long-term management strategies. For example, individuals likely to
sustain disease control will benefit from increased autonomy and flexibility and may be suited to remote monitoring
or patient-initiated follow-up pathways. Higher-risk individuals may be appropriate for adjunct therapies with closer
monitoring, facilitating earlier intervention for flares to reduce disease impact.
Dissemination
My findings will be disseminated widely through high-impact peer-reviewed publications and presentations at
national and international conferences. I will co-develop Plain English Summaries and infographics with my PPI
collaborators, which will be shared via UK and European psoriasis patient associations, newsletters, and social
media platforms to maximise p

rategies co-designed by patients and clinicians are preferable to a blanket
approach.
Participants were enthusiastic about the idea of a risk stratification tool providing individualised estimates of the
likelihood of sustained disease control on systemic therapy. The group highlighted the following key points, which
have shaped my proposal:
 Disease control maintained over the longer term, i.e. ≥6 months, is especially important. Hence, Phases 1
and 2 focus on sustaining disease control for ≥6 months as a key outcome.
 Not everyone will be comfortable with genetic data being used. However, the group agreed that there is
value in exploring whether genetic data can improve model performance. Consequently, Phase 2 will
compare models including and excluding genetic data.
 To maximise the tool’s clinical utility, early involvement of patients and clinicians is essential. This
influenced Phase 3 of my proposal, in which stakeholder engagement is central to model refinement, user
interface design and beta testing.
Following this event, 4 individuals expressed an interest in continued involvement in the project as part of a Patient
and Lay Advisory Group. They have also provided helpf

criteria.name: Integration of Lived Experience in Research Design
criteria.definition: How input from patients, carers, or people with relevant lived experience has shaped the research questions, design, and methods.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of consultation or co-design activities
Specific examples of changes made to study design based on lived experience
References to patient or community advisory groups

criteria.pros: The application demonstrates exceptional integration of lived experience. Evidence shows that PPI feedback directly shaped the research questions, outcome definitions, and the inclusion of genetic data (secppi__001_b, secppi__001_c). The design includes co-facilitated focus groups and iterative tool co-design with patients (secsa__001_c, secdrp__001_w). A specific PPI advisory event was held to refine the proposal before submission (secarb__001_e).
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the evidence for integration is comprehensive and spans the entire research lifecycle from conception to dissemination.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the evidence for integration is comprehensive and spans the entire research lifecycle from conception to dissemination.

evidence.text:
mic and clinical interests. I
became a trainee representative for the British Association of Dermatologists Biologics and Immunomodulators
Register (BADBIR), a national registry for people with psoriasis on systemic therapy. In addition, I have also
recently joined the BAD Biologic Therapy for Psoriasis Guideline Development Group as a trainee representative.
These experiences have highlighted critical unmet needs in psoriasis research, and have provided inspiration for
this DRF proposal, which has been subsequently refined through discussions with supervisors and PPI
collaborators.
Teaching Experience
I have taught medical students in classroom, ward and outpatient settings. I also designed and implemented a
well-received junior doctor-led teaching programme for medical students during my AFP. Additionally, I have
completed a Developing Teaching and Learning for Medical Educators course, and have supervised junior
colleagues for multiple audits and quality improvement projects.
Leadership and Management Experience
During my AFP, I sat on the Steering Committee of a national conference for junior doctors and medical students
to showcase their research. Within my department, I chair

ed weekly clinicopathological correlation meetings over a
6-month period, and I am currently responsible for organising monthly regional complex cases meetings.
Public Engagement Experience
I recently organised a PPI advisory event, collecting valuable feedback on my project proposal. I hope to utilise
this fellowship to build further experience in public engagement and involvement, supported by the extensive
expertise within our group.
Fellowship Impact
This fellowship provides the perfect platform to advance my career as an academic dermatologist, through an
ambitious project which could transform long-term management for people with psoriasis on systemic therapy. At
this pivotal stage in my training, taking an extended period away from clinical work will allow me to concentrate
fully on my growth as a researcher. I am confident that I will be able to maximise this opportunity, due to my robust
training plan, my expert supervisory team, and the dynamic research environment at KCL. Completing this
fellowship will position me well for a Clinical Lectureship, and bring me one step closer to my goal of becoming an
impactful Clinician Scientist.

evidence.text:
d evidence on the proportion of individuals sustaining disease control over time and the
factors associated with this.
 Model the likelihood of sustaining disease control on systemic therapy using real-world datasets.
 Optimise the predictive models for use as a risk stratification tool, with patient and clinician input.
Methods and Timelines
Phase 1 (Months 0-9): Conduct a systematic review to determine the proportion of individuals sustaining psoriasis
control on systemic therapy over time, and the clinical and genetic factors associated with this.
Phase 2 (Months 7-24): Develop and validate predictive models of sustained disease control on systemic therapy
using two large-scale real-world datasets from the UK and Europe to ensure generalisability. I will also explore
whether including genetic data improves model performance.
Phase 3 (Months 19-36): Refine the predictive models into a risk stratification tool. Patient and clinician focus
groups will determine clinically acceptable levels of model performance, influence the definition of risk categories,
and guide user interface design. The tool will be beta tested via a clinician workshop.
PPI feedback has been integral to deve

loping this proposal. PPI collaborators will be closely involved throughout,
including assisting with recruitment strategies, co-facilitating focus groups, and helping with qualitative analysis.
They will also ensure the research is inclusive and relevant to diverse communities.
Impact
Successful delivery of the risk stratification tool could reduce patient anxiety, empower individuals to address
modifiable risk factors, and personalise long-term management strategies. For example, individuals likely to
sustain disease control will benefit from increased autonomy and flexibility and may be suited to remote monitoring
or patient-initiated follow-up pathways. Higher-risk individuals may be appropriate for adjunct therapies with closer
monitoring, facilitating earlier intervention for flares to reduce disease impact.
Dissemination
My findings will be disseminated widely through high-impact peer-reviewed publications and presentations at
national and international conferences. I will co-develop Plain English Summaries and infographics with my PPI
collaborators, which will be shared via UK and European psoriasis patient associations, newsletters, and social
media platforms to maximise p

signals.signal_text: Explicit mentions of team composition across gender, ethnicity, age, or other relevant characteristics
Statements about inclusion in hiring or team-building
Evidence of equitable task allocation and collaboration

criteria.pros: The PPI group is explicitly recruited to ensure diversity in age, ethnicity, location, and disease duration, and includes carers/family members (secppi__001_a). The team composition includes a diverse supervisory team with expertise in dermatology, research methods, and statistics (secac2__001).
criteria.drawbacks: While the PPI group diversity is well-described, explicit statements regarding the demographic diversity of the core research team (beyond the supervisors) or specific hiring strategies for inclusivity are less detailed compared to the PPI section.
criteria.rationale: While the PPI group diversity is well-described, explicit statements regarding the demographic diversity of the core research team (beyond the supervisors) or specific hiring strategies for inclusivity are less detailed compared to the PPI section.

evidence.text:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
I originally developed this proposal with the findings of the 2018 James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership13
involving patients, carers and clinicians in mind. This highlighted research into factors affecting sustained disease
control in psoriasis as a key unmet need, aligning with 3 of the top 10 priorities identified.
My proposal has since been refined further based on feedback from a PPI advisory group meeting I organised.
Participants were recruited from local networks and the Psoriasis Association, with diversity in terms of age,
ethnicity, geographical location and disease duration. They also had varied experiences of different topical and
systemic therapies for psoriasis.
All participants strongly supported my research question and felt that it was relevant. In particular, they
emphasised that:
 A better understanding of factors affecting sustained psoriasis control would reduce uncertainty for people
on systemic therapy.
 Identification of modifiable risk factors may empower individuals to make appropriate lifestyle changes.
 Personalised follow-up st

criteria.name: Commitment to Maximising Public and Patient Benefit
criteria.definition: Clarity and strength of the plan to ensure research outcomes meaningfully benefit patients, carers, and the public.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of dissemination, knowledge translation, or implementation plans
Clear links between study objectives and anticipated benefits
Emphasis on actionable outcomes or improvements in health/care

criteria.pros: A robust dissemination plan is outlined, targeting patients (Plain English Summaries, social media), clinicians (conferences, journals), and policymakers (guideline updates) (secdrp__001_z, secppi__001_d). The plan explicitly links study objectives to anticipated benefits like reduced anxiety and optimized NHS resource allocation (secpesor__001_a, secsa__001_c). PPI collaborators are included as co-authors and presenters to maximize public benefit (secppi__001_d).
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the plan is comprehensive and clearly links outputs to specific beneficiary groups.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the plan is comprehensive and clearly links outputs to specific beneficiary groups.

Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
PPI is integral to the success of my research, particularly given its potential to provide more autonomy and
flexibility for individuals likely to sustain disease control long-term. To this end, I will establish a Patient and Lay
Advisory Group of 6 individuals, including people with psoriasis and their carers/family members. Individuals will
be recruited from my initial PPI session, the Psoriasis Association and the well-established inflammatory skin
disease PPI network led by my supervisors at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology. This will ensure a diversity of
views, backgrounds and experiences.
The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings around key decision points. Based on feedback, meetings
will be virtual to minimise disruption to daily schedules and maximise inclusivity. To reduce the potential for digital
exclusion, I will arrange practice online calls and provide phone-in options.
PPI collaborators will:
 Provide input on variabl

e selection for model development in Phase 2.
 Inform recruitment strategies for Phase 3, especially for underserved groups.
 Help design a topic guide and co-facilitate the focus groups in Phase 3.
 Assist with interpreting research findings and opine on their relevance to people with psoriasis and the
public.
Additionally, they will review communications on research findings intended for patients and the public. Guided by
the NIHR equality, diversity and inclusion toolkit, these will be widely disseminated via charities, established
European patient networks and partners, newsletters and social media. I will also leverage the annual national PPI
webinars co-organised by the Psoriasis Association and St John’s DermAcademy (>100 attendees per event) to
share my results. Where appropriate, I will invite PPI collaborators to co-author peer-reviewed publications and to
co-present at events.
PPI collaborators will be invited to attend PPI training sessions run by Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Trust and by the
NIHR Imperial College and Partners Research Support Service Hub. For those involved in Phase 3, I will provide
additional training on conducting focus groups and interpreting qualit

criteria.name: Inclusivity Across the Research Lifecycle
criteria.definition: Demonstration that inclusion is considered at all stages of the project: planning, recruitment, conduct, analysis, and dissemination.

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

criteria.pros: Inclusivity is demonstrated across the full research lifecycle: PPI involvement spans proposal refinement, variable selection, recruitment strategies, focus group facilitation, and interpretation of results (secppi__001_b, secppi__001_c). The plan ensures relevance to diverse communities at every stage, from design to dissemination (secpesor__001_c, secsa__001_c).
criteria.drawbacks: The plan is very strong; the only minor limitation is that the specific mechanisms for ongoing evaluation and adaptation of inclusion strategies during the project execution are implied rather than explicitly detailed as a separate monitoring process.
criteria.rationale: The plan is very strong; the only minor limitation is that the specific mechanisms for ongoing evaluation and adaptation of inclusion strategies during the project execution are implied rather than explicitly detailed as a separate monitoring process.

evidence.text:
o address has been identified as a research priority by people with psoriasis, their
families and friends, and the healthcare professionals who treat them. The design of the project incorporates
further feedback from a meeting I had with people with psoriasis. For example, they emphasised that involving
patients and healthcare professionals from the start is essential for developing a useful tool. Patients and the
public will continue to be involved during this project through 6-monthly meetings. I will invite them to help plan the
group discussions and analyse the conversations after the sessions. They will also ensure the research is
inclusive and relevant to diverse communities.
Impact
This work will help people better understand how likely they are to keep their psoriasis under control while
receiving a tablet or injection treatment. It will also allow healthcare professionals to provide better care matched to
individual needs. This will improve the patient experience and allow healthcare resources to be used more
efficiently.
Dissemination
I will share my findings by presenting at conferences and by publishing in scientific journals. I will also work with
patients and the publ
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
loping this proposal. PPI collaborators will be closely involved throughout,
including assisting with recruitment strategies, co-facilitating focus groups, and helping with qualitative analysis.
They will also ensure the research is inclusive and relevant to diverse communities.
Impact
Successful delivery of the risk stratification tool could reduce patient anxiety, empower individuals to address
modifiable risk factors, and personalise long-term management strategies. For example, individuals likely to
sustain disease control will benefit from increased autonomy and flexibility and may be suited to remote monitoring
or patient-initiated follow-up pathways. Higher-risk individuals may be appropriate for adjunct therapies with closer
monitoring, facilitating earlier intervention for flares to reduce disease impact.
Dissemination
My findings will be disseminated widely through high-impact peer-reviewed publications and presentations at
national and international conferences. I will co-develop Plain English Summaries and infographics with my PPI
collaborators, which will be shared via UK and European psoriasis patient associations, newsletters, and social
media platforms to maximise p

rategies co-designed by patients and clinicians are preferable to a blanket
approach.
Participants were enthusiastic about the idea of a risk stratification tool providing individualised estimates of the
likelihood of sustained disease control on systemic therapy. The group highlighted the following key points, which
have shaped my proposal:
 Disease control maintained over the longer term, i.e. ≥6 months, is especially important. Hence, Phases 1
and 2 focus on sustaining disease control for ≥6 months as a key outcome.
 Not everyone will be comfortable with genetic data being used. However, the group agreed that there is
value in exploring whether genetic data can improve model performance. Consequently, Phase 2 will
compare models including and excluding genetic data.
 To maximise the tool’s clinical utility, early involvement of patients and clinicians is essential. This
influenced Phase 3 of my proposal, in which stakeholder engagement is central to model refinement, user
interface design and beta testing.
Following this event, 4 individuals expressed an interest in continued involvement in the project as part of a Patient
and Lay Advisory Group. They have also provided helpf

ul feedback for the proposal’s Plain English Summary.

views, backgrounds and experiences.

sub_criteria.name: Use of Formatting, Headings, and Subheadings
sub_criteria.definition: Effective use of text formatting to make the application clear, navigable, and easy to read.

signals.signal_text: Presence and consistency of headings and subheadings
Use of bold or emphasis for key terms
Structured hierarchy of sections (logical nesting of headings)
Patterns detected via text parsing for formatting markers

sub_criteria.pros: The Application Form Analysis confirms a robust structural hierarchy with 39 numbered heading-like lines and 10 distinct parser sections, ensuring clear navigation. It also detects 38 extracted emphasis markers and 26 list markers, indicating effective use of formatting to highlight key terms and structure content.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While 38 emphasis markers were detected, the analysis notes a parser limitation where bold/emphasis may be lost during text extraction; therefore, the score for emphasis usage is capped at 4 to account for potential under-detection rather than absence.
sub_criteria.rationale: While 38 emphasis markers were detected, the analysis notes a parser limitation where bold/emphasis may be lost during text extraction; therefore, the score for emphasis usage is capped at 4 to account for potential under-detection rather than absence.

signals.signal_text: Semantic similarity measures between sentences/paragraphs

Novelty score for each section (unique content vs. overlap)
